  2. Chapter 3: Importing & Exploring Data

Chapter 3: Importing & Exploring Data

read_csv, tibbles, exploring data structure, and handling missing values

Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

  • Import CSV files with readr::read_csv() and Excel files with readxl::read_excel()
  • Explain the difference between a tibble and a base R data frame
  • Use glimpse(), summary(), and str() to explore data structure before analysis
  • Systematically diagnose missing values — how many, which columns, what patterns
  • Access individual columns with $ and create new tibbles from scratch

Key Concepts

CSV and Excel Import

read_csv() from the readr package (part of tidyverse) reads comma-separated files and automatically guesses column types. read_excel() from the readxl package handles .xlsx and .xls files. Both return tibbles. Always inspect the result immediately after import — column types may not be what you expect.

Tibbles

A tibble is the tidyverse version of a data frame — a rectangular table where each column is a vector and columns can have different types. Tibbles print more neatly (showing only the first 10 rows and as many columns as fit on screen), never convert strings to factors automatically, and warn you when you access a column that does not exist.

Data Exploration

Before any analysis, you must understand your data’s shape and content. glimpse() shows every column’s name, type, and first few values. summary() gives five-number summaries for numeric columns and frequency counts for factors. nrow() and ncol() tell you the dimensions. This exploration step catches data quality issues early.

Missing Values in Imported Data

Real-world health datasets — DHIS2 extracts, facility registers, survey exports — always have missing values. They may appear as blank cells, “NA”, “N/A”, “.”, “-”, or “99”. The read_csv() function recognizes NA and blank cells by default, but you may need to specify additional codes with na = c("", "NA", "N/A", ".", "-"). Always quantify missingness before proceeding.

Lecture Slides

Open slides full-screen →

R Code Workshop

This workshop simulates importing and exploring a facility-level MNH dataset — the kind of data you might receive from a Ministry of Health or extract from DHIS2.

Importing a CSV File

library(tidyverse)

# Import facility-level MNH data
# In practice, replace the path with your actual file location
facility_data <- read_csv("data/facility_data.csv")

# read_csv prints a column specification showing what types it guessed
# Always check this output for unexpected types

# For Excel files, use readxl
# library(readxl)
# facility_data <- read_excel("data/facility_data.xlsx", sheet = "Sheet1")

# Specify custom NA codes if your data uses them
# facility_data <- read_csv("data/facility_data.csv",
#                           na = c("", "NA", "N/A", ".", "-", "99"))

When you run read_csv(), it prints a message showing which column types it guessed. If a column you expected to be numeric shows as character, that usually means there is a non-numeric value hiding somewhere in that column. Investigate before proceeding.

Exploring Data Structure

# Dimensions: how many rows (facilities) and columns (variables)?
nrow(facility_data)
ncol(facility_data)
dim(facility_data)

# glimpse() — best quick overview (shows every column)
glimpse(facility_data)

# summary() — numeric summaries + NA counts
summary(facility_data)

# First and last few rows
head(facility_data)
tail(facility_data)

# Column names
names(facility_data)

Make glimpse() your first command after every import. It tells you at a glance whether columns have the right types, whether character columns have unexpected values, and how many observations you are working with. In MNH work, always verify that the number of rows matches the expected number of facilities or reporting periods.

Diagnosing Missing Values

# Count NAs in every column
colSums(is.na(facility_data))

# Percentage missing per column
round(colMeans(is.na(facility_data)) * 100, 1)

# Total missing cells in the entire dataset
sum(is.na(facility_data))

# Which rows have any missing data?
facility_data[!complete.cases(facility_data), ]

# How many complete rows (no NAs at all)?
sum(complete.cases(facility_data))

In MNH datasets, missingness is rarely random. Facilities that fail to report are often the ones with the fewest staff, the weakest infrastructure, and potentially the worst outcomes. Before you drop missing rows, ask yourself: “Would removing these facilities bias my results toward healthier-looking facilities?”

Accessing Columns and Creating Tibbles

# Access a single column with $
facility_data$facility_name
facility_data$total_deliveries

# Quick column-level checks
unique(facility_data$country)
table(facility_data$facility_type)
range(facility_data$nmr, na.rm = TRUE)

# Create a tibble from scratch (useful for reference tables)
country_targets <- tibble(
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Kenya", "Rwanda"),
  nmr_target = c(15, 15, 15, 12, 12),
  mmr_target = c(200, 200, 200, 150, 150),
  target_year = rep(2030, 5)
)
country_targets

The $ operator extracts a single column as a vector. The table() function gives you a quick frequency count — useful for checking how many facilities of each type exist, or how many observations come from each country.

Saving Clean Data

# After initial inspection and cleaning, save a clean version
# write_csv(facility_data, "data/facility_data_clean.csv")

# For R-specific format (preserves factor levels, date types, etc.)
# saveRDS(facility_data, "data/facility_data_clean.rds")
# facility_data <- readRDS("data/facility_data_clean.rds")

Save your cleaned data separately from the raw import. This way you always have the original file as a reference, and your cleaning steps are documented in the script that produced the clean version.

Key Takeaways

Import with read_csv(), explore with glimpse(). These two functions bookend your data loading workflow. Always inspect column types and dimensions immediately after import.

Quantify missingness before analysis. Count NAs per column, calculate the percentage missing, and ask whether the missingness pattern could bias your results.

Tibbles are your default data container. They print cleanly, warn about mistakes, and integrate seamlessly with the tidyverse tools you will learn next.

Looking Ahead

Next in Chapter 4 — Data Transformation with dplyr, you will learn to reshape and summarize your data using five core verbs:

  • filter() — select rows based on conditions
  • select() — choose specific columns
  • mutate() — create or modify columns
  • summarise() — compute summary statistics
  • group_by() — apply operations within groups (by country, by facility type, by quarter)
